title: Rotting Oranges
code: int rows = grid.Length, cols = grid[0].Length, fresh = 0, minutes = 0;
var q = new Queue<(int r, int c)>();
if (grid[r][c] == 2) q.Enqueue((r, c)); // all initial sources
for (int i = q.Count; i > 0; i--)    // drain exactly one layer
int nr = r + d[0], nc = c + d[1];
if (nr >= 0 && nr < rows && nc >= 0 && nc < cols && grid[nr][nc] == 1)

q.label: BFS frontier (rotten cells, one ring at a time)

frames.caption: Seed the queue with all rotten cells. One source at (0,0); 6 fresh oranges to reach.
frames.note: Multi-source BFS starts not from one vertex but from ALL sources simultaneously. We scan the whole grid and enqueue every already-rotten cell at minute 0, while counting the fresh oranges we must still reach. Seeding all sources together is the trick that makes one BFS compute, for every cell, the distance to its NEAREST source at once.

frames.caption: Minute 1: drain the current ring of 1 rotten cell(s) and rot their fresh neighbors.
frames.note: Each outer iteration is one minute of real time, so we must drain EXACTLY the cells already in the queue — one full BFS ring — before the freshly-rotted cells (added this minute) get their turn next minute. Snapshotting q.Count at the top of the minute is what keeps layers from bleeding into each other; this is the multi-source analogue of BFS processing vertices in nondecreasing distance order.

frames.caption: Process (0,0): rot fresh neighbor(s) (1,0), (0,1). fresh → 4.
frames.note: This neighbor is fresh and in bounds, so the rot reaches it for the FIRST time on this minute — which is necessarily the soonest any source could (every step costs 1 minute). We rot it now, decrement the fresh count, and enqueue it so it spreads on the NEXT ring. Marking it rotten immediately is exactly what stops it from being processed twice.

frames.caption: Minute 2: drain the current ring of 2 rotten cell(s) and rot their fresh neighbors.
frames.note: Each outer iteration is one minute of real time, so we must drain EXACTLY the cells already in the queue — one full BFS ring — before the freshly-rotted cells (added this minute) get their turn next minute. Snapshotting q.Count at the top of the minute is what keeps layers from bleeding into each other; this is the multi-source analogue of BFS processing vertices in nondecreasing distance order.

frames.caption: Process (1,0): rot fresh neighbor(s) (1,1). fresh → 3.
frames.note: This neighbor is fresh and in bounds, so the rot reaches it for the FIRST time on this minute — which is necessarily the soonest any source could (every step costs 1 minute). We rot it now, decrement the fresh count, and enqueue it so it spreads on the NEXT ring. Marking it rotten immediately is exactly what stops it from being processed twice.

frames.caption: Process (0,1): rot fresh neighbor(s) (0,2). fresh → 2.
frames.note: This neighbor is fresh and in bounds, so the rot reaches it for the FIRST time on this minute — which is necessarily the soonest any source could (every step costs 1 minute). We rot it now, decrement the fresh count, and enqueue it so it spreads on the NEXT ring. Marking it rotten immediately is exactly what stops it from being processed twice.

frames.caption: Minute 3: drain the current ring of 2 rotten cell(s) and rot their fresh neighbors.
frames.note: Each outer iteration is one minute of real time, so we must drain EXACTLY the cells already in the queue — one full BFS ring — before the freshly-rotted cells (added this minute) get their turn next minute. Snapshotting q.Count at the top of the minute is what keeps layers from bleeding into each other; this is the multi-source analogue of BFS processing vertices in nondecreasing distance order.

frames.caption: Process (1,1): rot fresh neighbor(s) (2,1). fresh → 1.
frames.note: This neighbor is fresh and in bounds, so the rot reaches it for the FIRST time on this minute — which is necessarily the soonest any source could (every step costs 1 minute). We rot it now, decrement the fresh count, and enqueue it so it spreads on the NEXT ring. Marking it rotten immediately is exactly what stops it from being processed twice.

frames.caption: Process (0,2): no in-bounds fresh neighbor left — nothing to rot.
frames.note: A neighbor that is out of bounds, empty (0), or already rotten (2) contributes nothing — we skip it. Bounds-checking 0 <= nr < R && 0 <= nc < C before reading, and ignoring non-fresh cells, is what keeps grid BFS at O(R*C): each cell is rotted once and each of its 4 edges inspected once.

frames.caption: Minute 4: drain the current ring of 1 rotten cell(s) and rot their fresh neighbors.
frames.note: Each outer iteration is one minute of real time, so we must drain EXACTLY the cells already in the queue — one full BFS ring — before the freshly-rotted cells (added this minute) get their turn next minute. Snapshotting q.Count at the top of the minute is what keeps layers from bleeding into each other; this is the multi-source analogue of BFS processing vertices in nondecreasing distance order.

frames.caption: Process (2,1): rot fresh neighbor(s) (2,2). fresh → 0.
frames.note: This neighbor is fresh and in bounds, so the rot reaches it for the FIRST time on this minute — which is necessarily the soonest any source could (every step costs 1 minute). We rot it now, decrement the fresh count, and enqueue it so it spreads on the NEXT ring. Marking it rotten immediately is exactly what stops it from being processed twice.

frames.caption: No fresh oranges remain — every orange rotted in 4 minutes. Return 4.
frames.note: fresh has hit 0, so every reachable orange is now rotten and the loop stops. The answer is the number of minutes (BFS layers) that elapsed — the distance from the nearest source to the LAST orange to rot. Had any fresh orange been walled off from all sources, fresh would stay > 0 and we would return -1 instead.
